content: An epidemic curve (epi curve) is a histogram showing case counts by date of symptom onset, revealing outbreak patterns. Three main types exist: point source (sharp peak, rapid decline—single exposure like foodborne illness), propagated (successive waves growing taller—person-to-person transmission), and continuous common source (sustained plateau—ongoing exposure like contaminated water). The shape indicates transmission mode, incubation period (time from exposure to peak), and outbreak magnitude. Epi curves help identify the exposure period and evaluate intervention effectiveness.
source: CDC outbreak investigation guidelines, WHO field epidemiology manual

content: Constructing an epi curve requires: accurate case definitions, symptom onset dates (not report dates), and appropriate time intervals (typically 1/4 to 1/3 of incubation period). Analysis steps: identify index case, determine outbreak pattern, estimate incubation period from exposure to peak, calculate attack rates by exposure category. For point source outbreaks, all cases occur within one incubation period. Propagated outbreaks show generations of cases with intervals matching serial interval. Interventions should target identified transmission mode: remove contamination source for point source, interrupt chains for propagated.

Epi curve interpretation requires considering: right-censoring (recent cases not yet reported), changes in testing or surveillance, and importation versus local transmission. Secondary attack rate measures person-to-person spread: cases among contacts divided by total contacts. Outbreak duration relative to incubation period distinguishes point source (<2 incubation periods) from propagated (>3 generations). Mixed patterns occur when point source triggers secondary propagation. Comparing epi curves across subpopulations reveals risk factors and transmission settings.
source: Field Epidemiology Manual (WHO), CDC training materials
